sections.label: Standard Tune-Up Question
sections.content: Our standard tune-up is {maintenance_fee}. That covers a full system inspection, cleaning, and calibration by a licensed technician to make sure everything is running the way it should before the season hits.
sections.groqContent:
A tune-up is a comprehensive preventive maintenance visit where a licensed HVAC technician inspects, cleans, and optimizes the entire system to keep it running at peak performance. The visit typically costs {maintenance_fee} and takes about 45 to 90 minutes depending on the system type and condition.

During the visit, the technician works through a structured checklist designed to evaluate every critical component. On the cooling side, that includes checking refrigerant levels and pressures, inspecting the condenser coil for dirt and debris, cleaning the evaporator coil if accessible, testing the compressor amperage and capacitor values with a multimeter, verifying contactor operation, checking the condensate drain line for clogs, inspecting electrical connections and tightening any loose terminals, and measuring the temperature split between supply and return air. On the heating side, the technician inspects the heat exchanger for cracks or corrosion, tests ignition sequence and flame sensor operation, checks the gas valve and gas pressure, inspects the inducer motor and pressure switch, and evaluates the flue and venting for proper draft.

Beyond the mechanical inspection, the technician also evaluates airflow by checking the blower wheel for buildup, inspecting the air filter and recommending replacement if needed, and verifying that return and supply vents are unobstructed. The thermostat is tested for calibration accuracy, ensuring the system cycles correctly and maintains the set temperature within a reasonable range.

The purpose of all this is to catch small issues before they become expensive failures. A weak capacitor found during a tune-up costs a fraction of what it costs when it fails on the hottest day of summer. A cracked heat exchanger found during fall maintenance prevents a carbon monoxide risk during winter. Regular maintenance also keeps the system running efficiently, which directly impacts monthly energy costs. A well-maintained system uses less electricity to produce the same output compared to a neglected one.

Most manufacturers recommend annual maintenance to keep warranties valid. Skipping maintenance can void coverage on major components like compressors and heat exchangers, leaving the homeowner responsible for the full replacement cost.

If the caller is ready to schedule, the next step is collecting their name, phone number, and address. Maintenance visits are typically scheduled in advance during regular business hours.

sections.label: What Is Included In A Tune-Up
sections.content: A tune-up includes a full inspection of the electrical, mechanical, and safety components. The technician checks refrigerant levels, cleans coils, tests capacitors, verifies airflow, and calibrates the thermostat. Everything gets documented.
sections.groqContent:
During a maintenance visit, the technician works through a comprehensive checklist that covers every major component and system function. The visit typically takes 45 to 90 minutes depending on the system type, age, and whether one or both units are being serviced.

The outdoor unit inspection includes cleaning the condenser coil with a specialized coil cleaner and low-pressure water rinse, inspecting the condenser fan motor and blade for damage or wobble, testing the compressor amperage against the rated value on the data plate, measuring run and start capacitor values and comparing to rated specifications, checking the contactor for pitting or carbon buildup on the contact points, inspecting the electrical disconnect and wiring for damage or corrosion, verifying that the unit pad is level and stable, and clearing vegetation and debris from around the unit to maintain proper airflow clearance.

The indoor unit inspection includes checking the evaporator coil for dirt and ice buildup, clearing the condensate drain line and verifying proper drainage, inspecting the drain pan and float switch for proper operation, testing the blower motor for proper amperage and operation, inspecting the blower wheel for dirt accumulation that reduces airflow, checking all electrical connections and tightening loose terminals, inspecting the air filter and recommending replacement if dirty, testing thermostat calibration and cycling, and measuring the temperature split between supply and return air.

For heating systems, additional checks include inspecting the heat exchanger for cracks or corrosion, testing the ignition system through its full sequence, cleaning the flame sensor, checking gas pressure at the manifold and supply, inspecting burners for carbon buildup, testing the inducer motor and pressure switch, and inspecting the flue and venting.

The technician documents every measurement and observation. When the inspection is complete, they walk the homeowner through their findings, explain what is operating well and what may need attention, and provide recommendations with estimated costs for any repairs that are not part of the standard tune-up scope. There is no obligation to approve any additional work.

This checklist-driven approach ensures nothing is missed and gives the homeowner a complete picture of their system's health.

sections.label: AC Tune-Up Specifically
sections.content: For the cooling system, the technician inspects the condenser coil, checks refrigerant pressures, tests the compressor and capacitor, clears the drain line, and measures the temperature split to make sure the AC is performing the way it should.
sections.groqContent:
An AC tune-up focuses specifically on the cooling system and is ideally performed before the cooling season begins, typically in spring. The goal is to make sure every component of the cooling cycle is clean, calibrated, and operating within manufacturer specifications so the system runs efficiently and reliably through the hottest months.

The technician starts with the outdoor condenser unit. They inspect and clean the condenser coil, which collects dirt, grass clippings, and debris over time. A dirty condenser coil forces the compressor to work harder, increasing energy consumption and accelerating wear on the most expensive component in the system. The technician checks the condenser fan motor for proper operation, tests the compressor amperage draw against the rated load amps on the data plate, and measures both the run and start capacitor values. A capacitor that has drifted more than 5 percent from its rated microfarad value is flagged for replacement because it will eventually fail, often during peak demand.

Moving inside, the technician inspects the evaporator coil for dirt buildup and ice formation. They check the condensate drain line by clearing any blockage to prevent water backups that can trigger safety shutoffs or cause water damage. The blower motor and blower wheel are inspected for balance and cleanliness. A dirty blower wheel reduces airflow significantly, which affects cooling performance and can lead to frozen evaporator coils.

Electrical connections throughout the system are inspected and tightened. Loose connections create resistance that generates heat, which degrades wiring over time and can cause intermittent failures. The thermostat is tested for temperature accuracy and proper cycling behavior.

The technician measures the temperature difference between supply and return air. A healthy AC system should produce a 15 to 20 degree temperature split. Anything outside that range indicates a performance issue that can be addressed before it worsens.

Refrigerant levels are checked by measuring suction and discharge pressures. If the system is low, it typically indicates a leak that should be addressed rather than simply adding refrigerant, which is a temporary fix.

When the tune-up is complete, the technician reviews their findings and flags anything that needs attention, giving the homeowner time to plan and budget for any recommended repairs.

sections.label: Heating Tune-Up Specifically
sections.content: For the heating system, the technician inspects the heat exchanger, tests the ignition sequence, cleans the flame sensor, checks gas pressure, and evaluates the flue and venting. That covers the safety and performance side of things.
sections.groqContent:
A heating tune-up focuses specifically on the furnace or heat pump and is ideally performed in the fall before cold weather arrives. The purpose is to verify safe operation, maximize efficiency, and catch any issues before the system is relied on daily for warmth.

The most critical component the technician checks is the heat exchanger. This is the metal chamber inside the furnace where combustion gases are separated from the air circulating through the home. A cracked or corroded heat exchanger can leak carbon monoxide into the living space, which is a serious safety hazard. The technician performs a visual inspection and may use specialized tools to detect hairline cracks that are not visible to the eye. Any heat exchanger issue is flagged immediately because it requires prompt attention.

The ignition system is tested through its full sequence. For systems with a hot surface igniter, the technician checks the igniter for cracks and measures its resistance to ensure it will glow hot enough to light the burners reliably. For systems with a pilot light, the pilot flame is checked for proper size, color, and position relative to the thermocouple or flame sensor. The flame sensor itself is cleaned to remove oxidation buildup, which is one of the most common causes of furnace shutdowns during the heating season. A dirty flame sensor cannot detect the burner flame, causing the control board to shut down the gas valve as a safety measure.

The gas valve is tested for proper gas pressure on both the manifold and supply side. The burners are inspected for corrosion, carbon buildup, or misalignment that could affect combustion efficiency or create uneven flame patterns. The inducer motor, which pulls combustion gases through the heat exchanger and out the flue, is tested for proper airflow and motor integrity. The pressure switch that monitors inducer airflow is verified for correct operation.

The blower motor and blower wheel are inspected for cleanliness and proper operation, just as in a cooling tune-up. The air filter is checked and the thermostat is tested in heating mode to confirm the system cycles correctly. The flue pipe and venting are inspected for proper draft, blockages, or separation at joints.

The technician reviews all findings with the homeowner and flags anything that needs attention before the heating season begins.

sections.label: How Often Should I Get Maintenance
sections.content: Most manufacturers recommend at least once a year. If you have both heating and cooling, twice a year is ideal — one before summer and one before winter. That keeps both sides of the system in good shape.
sections.groqContent:
Regular maintenance is the single most effective way to protect an HVAC system from unexpected breakdowns, reduce energy costs, and extend the equipment's useful life. The comparison to automotive maintenance is direct — just as a car that never gets oil changes will eventually suffer engine damage, an HVAC system that never gets serviced will degrade faster and fail sooner.

The efficiency impact is measurable. A dirty condenser coil alone can reduce cooling efficiency by 20 to 30 percent, which translates directly into higher electric bills. A blower wheel caked with dust reduces airflow by 10 to 15 percent, forcing the system to run longer cycles to reach the set temperature. A capacitor that has drifted from its rated value makes the compressor work harder on every startup, shortening its lifespan. These are all conditions that develop gradually and go unnoticed without professional inspection.

The cost comparison between maintenance and emergency repair is significant. A tune-up costs a fraction of what a failed compressor, a cracked heat exchanger, or a seized blower motor costs to replace. Most of the common failures that leave homeowners without cooling or heating in extreme weather could have been caught and addressed during a routine maintenance visit for a fraction of the emergency repair cost.

Breakdown prevention is especially important during peak demand. When summer temperatures hit triple digits or winter cold drops below freezing, HVAC companies receive the highest volume of emergency calls. Response times stretch, costs increase, and parts availability tightens. A system that has been maintained is far less likely to fail during these critical periods because the components most prone to failure have already been inspected and replaced if necessary.

System lifespan is directly tied to maintenance frequency. A well-maintained system can operate reliably for 15 to 20 years or more. A neglected system may start experiencing frequent failures after 8 to 10 years, leading to premature replacement at a cost of thousands of dollars. The annual investment in maintenance is small relative to the replacement cost.

Most HVAC manufacturers explicitly require annual professional maintenance to keep warranty coverage active on major components like compressors and heat exchangers.

sections.label: Benefits Of Regular Maintenance
sections.content: Regular maintenance keeps your system running efficiently, catches small issues before they become big repairs, extends the equipment lifespan, and helps protect your manufacturer warranty. It is the best way to avoid surprises.
sections.groqContent:
Regular maintenance is the single most effective way to protect an HVAC system from unexpected breakdowns, reduce energy costs, and extend the equipment's useful life. The comparison to automotive maintenance is direct — just as a car that never gets oil changes will eventually suffer engine damage, an HVAC system that never gets serviced will degrade faster and fail sooner.

The efficiency impact is measurable. A dirty condenser coil alone can reduce cooling efficiency by 20 to 30 percent, which translates directly into higher electric bills. A blower wheel caked with dust reduces airflow by 10 to 15 percent, forcing the system to run longer cycles to reach the set temperature. A capacitor that has drifted from its rated value makes the compressor work harder on every startup, shortening its lifespan. These are all conditions that develop gradually and go unnoticed without professional inspection.

The cost comparison between maintenance and emergency repair is significant. A tune-up costs a fraction of what a failed compressor, a cracked heat exchanger, or a seized blower motor costs to replace. Most of the common failures that leave homeowners without cooling or heating in extreme weather could have been caught and addressed during a routine maintenance visit for a fraction of the emergency repair cost.

Breakdown prevention is especially important during peak demand. When summer temperatures hit triple digits or winter cold drops below freezing, HVAC companies receive the highest volume of emergency calls. Response times stretch, costs increase, and parts availability tightens. A system that has been maintained is far less likely to fail during these critical periods because the components most prone to failure have already been inspected and replaced if necessary.

System lifespan is directly tied to maintenance frequency. A well-maintained system can operate reliably for 15 to 20 years or more. A neglected system may start experiencing frequent failures after 8 to 10 years, leading to premature replacement at a cost of thousands of dollars. The annual investment in maintenance is small relative to the replacement cost.

Most HVAC manufacturers explicitly require annual professional maintenance to keep warranty coverage active on major components like compressors and heat exchangers.

sections.label: Maintenance And Warranty Requirements
sections.content: Most manufacturers require annual professional maintenance to keep warranty coverage active on major components. Skipping it can give them grounds to deny a claim if something fails, even while still in the warranty period.
sections.groqContent:
Most HVAC manufacturers include a maintenance requirement in their warranty terms. The standard language requires that the system receive professional maintenance at least once per year to keep warranty coverage active on major components. Failing to maintain the system does not automatically void the entire warranty, but it can give the manufacturer grounds to deny a claim on a specific component if they determine that lack of maintenance contributed to the failure.

The components most commonly covered under extended warranties include the compressor, heat exchanger, and in some cases the evaporator coil. These are also the most expensive components in the system to replace. A compressor replacement can cost several thousand dollars for parts and labor. A heat exchanger replacement on a furnace is often close to the cost of a new furnace entirely. These are exactly the components a homeowner wants warranty protection on, and exactly the ones most at risk if annual maintenance is skipped.

For newer systems, the warranty coverage is typically strongest in the first five to ten years. This is also the period when maintaining the system is most critical for preserving that coverage. A system that is only two or three years old and experiences a compressor failure would normally be covered under the parts warranty. But if the homeowner cannot show a record of annual professional maintenance, the manufacturer may deny the claim and the homeowner is responsible for the full cost.

For older systems beyond the warranty period, maintenance is still valuable because it catches wear and degradation that accelerates as components age. A 12-year-old system that has been maintained annually is typically in significantly better condition than a 12-year-old system that has never been serviced. The maintained system's components have been cleaned, calibrated, and inspected regularly, so when something does wear out, it is caught before it causes a cascading failure that damages other components.

Keeping a record of annual maintenance visits also adds value to the home itself. A documented HVAC service history gives potential buyers confidence that the system has been properly cared for, which can be a selling point during a real estate transaction.

sections.label: Maintenance Plan Question
sections.content: We do have a maintenance plan. It includes two tune-ups per year, priority scheduling, and service calls are free with repairs. It is a good way to keep everything covered without having to remember to call each season.
sections.groqContent:
A maintenance plan is an annual agreement that includes scheduled tune-ups, priority service, and additional benefits that make it more cost-effective than paying for individual visits and services separately. The plan is designed for homeowners who want to protect their system year-round without having to remember to call and schedule each season.

The standard plan includes two professional tune-up visits per year, one for cooling before summer and one for heating before winter. Each visit follows the same comprehensive inspection checklist that a standalone tune-up includes, covering all mechanical, electrical, and safety components. The difference is that plan members do not pay separately for these visits because they are included in the annual plan cost.

Beyond the tune-ups, plan members receive priority scheduling, which means they move ahead of non-members when booking service appointments. During peak demand periods in summer and winter, this priority can mean the difference between getting a technician the same day versus waiting several days. For emergency repairs, plan members are typically seen first.

One of the most significant financial benefits is that service calls are free with repairs for plan members. That means the diagnostic fee is waived when a repair is approved during the visit, saving the member the full cost of the diagnostic. For a homeowner who needs even one repair per year, this benefit alone can offset a significant portion of the plan cost.

Some plans also include discounts on repairs and parts, typically ranging from 10 to 15 percent. Over the course of a year, especially for older systems that may need a part replaced, these discounts add up.

The plan is billed annually or monthly depending on the company's options. There is no long-term contract obligation in most cases — the member can cancel or choose not to renew when the term expires.

For callers comparing a single tune-up visit at {maintenance_fee} versus the annual plan, the plan typically breaks even with just the two included visits. Any repairs needed during the year that benefit from the waived diagnostic fee and parts discount push the plan into clear savings territory.

sections.label: Maintenance For Rental Property
sections.content: We work with landlords and property managers regularly. We can coordinate access with tenants, provide documentation for your records, and schedule multiple properties if needed. Just let us know the details.
sections.groqContent:
Maintenance visits are typically scheduled in advance during regular business hours. Unlike emergency repair calls that may be dispatched same-day, tune-ups are planned appointments that allow the scheduling team to assign the right technician and allocate the appropriate time window.

Availability depends on the season. The highest demand for cooling tune-ups is in spring as homeowners prepare for summer. The highest demand for heating tune-ups is in early fall before the first cold snap. Booking early in the season provides more flexibility in appointment times and avoids the rush that occurs when the first extreme weather event hits.

The typical appointment window is a morning or afternoon block. The actual tune-up takes 45 to 90 minutes depending on the system type and whether one or both units are being serviced. The technician needs access to both the indoor and outdoor equipment, so someone 18 or older should be present at the property to provide access and receive the findings report at the end.

For the outdoor unit, the technician needs clear access to the condenser, which means trimming any vegetation that is within 12 to 18 inches of the unit. For the indoor unit, the technician needs access to the air handler or furnace, which may be located in a closet, attic, garage, or utility room. If the unit is in the attic, a clear path with safe access is important.

The homeowner does not need to do anything special to prepare the system before the visit. The system should be in its normal operating state so the technician can observe real-world performance. If the system has been turned off for the season, the technician will start it up as part of the inspection.

After the visit, the technician reviews all findings and provides recommendations. If any additional work is suggested beyond the standard tune-up scope, it is presented as a separate recommendation with estimated costs. There is no obligation to approve anything beyond the scheduled maintenance.

Cancellation or rescheduling is straightforward. Calling at least 24 hours in advance allows the scheduling team to fill the slot and offer the caller a new appointment that works with their schedule.

sections.label: Maintenance With System Making Noise
sections.content: If your system is making an unusual noise, that is typically something that needs diagnosing rather than a standard tune-up. A tune-up follows a preventive checklist. A diagnostic visit focuses on identifying and fixing the specific issue.
sections.groqContent:
A tune-up and a diagnostic repair visit are two fundamentally different services with different purposes, different equipment, and different scheduling. Understanding the difference helps the caller get the right service for their situation and avoids wasting time and money on the wrong one.

A tune-up is preventive maintenance. It is a scheduled visit where the technician follows a set inspection checklist to clean, calibrate, and optimize a system that is already working. The goal is to keep it working well, catch small issues before they become expensive, and extend the equipment's life. Maintenance technicians follow the checklist, document their findings, and flag anything that needs attention. They are not dispatched to troubleshoot a specific problem.

A diagnostic repair visit is reactive. It is scheduled because the system has a specific issue — not cooling, making a noise, leaking water, tripping a breaker, or some other symptom. The technician arrives with diagnostic tools and common replacement parts specifically to identify the root cause and fix it. The repair truck is stocked differently than the maintenance truck. The technician's approach is different. The scheduling priority is different.

When a caller describes symptoms like no cooling, strange noises, water leaking, warm air from the vents, or a system that keeps shutting off, those are repair issues. Booking a maintenance visit for those situations is like scheduling an oil change when the car engine is overheating. sections.label: Maintenance Availability And Lead Time
sections.content: Maintenance visits are scheduled in advance, usually within a few days to a week depending on the season. Spring and fall fill up fastest, so booking early gives you the best selection of appointment times.
sections.groqContent:
Maintenance visits are typically scheduled in advance during regular business hours. Unlike emergency repair calls that may be dispatched same-day, tune-ups are planned appointments that allow the scheduling team to assign the right technician and allocate the appropriate time window.

Availability depends on the season. The highest demand for cooling tune-ups is in spring as homeowners prepare for summer. The highest demand for heating tune-ups is in early fall before the first cold snap. Booking early in the season provides more flexibility in appointment times and avoids the rush that occurs when the first extreme weather event hits.

The typical appointment window is a morning or afternoon block. The actual tune-up takes 45 to 90 minutes depending on the system type and whether one or both units are being serviced. The technician needs access to both the indoor and outdoor equipment, so someone 18 or older should be present at the property to provide access and receive the findings report at the end.

For the outdoor unit, the technician needs clear access to the condenser, which means trimming any vegetation that is within 12 to 18 inches of the unit. For the indoor unit, the technician needs access to the air handler or furnace, which may be located in a closet, attic, garage, or utility room. If the unit is in the attic, a clear path with safe access is important.

The homeowner does not need to do anything special to prepare the system before the visit. The system should be in its normal operating state so the technician can observe real-world performance. If the system has been turned off for the season, the technician will start it up as part of the inspection.

After the visit, the technician reviews all findings and provides recommendations. If any additional work is suggested beyond the standard tune-up scope, it is presented as a separate recommendation with estimated costs. There is no obligation to approve anything beyond the scheduled maintenance.

Cancellation or rescheduling is straightforward. Calling at least 24 hours in advance allows the scheduling team to fill the slot and offer the caller a new appointment that works with their schedule.

sections.label: What To Expect During The Visit
sections.content: The technician works through a complete inspection checklist, checks every major component, documents the findings, and walks you through what they found. If anything needs attention beyond the tune-up, they will let you know with no pressure.
sections.groqContent:
During a maintenance visit, the technician works through a comprehensive checklist that covers every major component and system function. The visit typically takes 45 to 90 minutes depending on the system type, age, and whether one or both units are being serviced.

The outdoor unit inspection includes cleaning the condenser coil with a specialized coil cleaner and low-pressure water rinse, inspecting the condenser fan motor and blade for damage or wobble, testing the compressor amperage against the rated value on the data plate, measuring run and start capacitor values and comparing to rated specifications, checking the contactor for pitting or carbon buildup on the contact points, inspecting the electrical disconnect and wiring for damage or corrosion, verifying that the unit pad is level and stable, and clearing vegetation and debris from around the unit to maintain proper airflow clearance.

The indoor unit inspection includes checking the evaporator coil for dirt and ice buildup, clearing the condensate drain line and verifying proper drainage, inspecting the drain pan and float switch for proper operation, testing the blower motor for proper amperage and operation, inspecting the blower wheel for dirt accumulation that reduces airflow, checking all electrical connections and tightening loose terminals, inspecting the air filter and recommending replacement if dirty, testing thermostat calibration and cycling, and measuring the temperature split between supply and return air.

For heating systems, additional checks include inspecting the heat exchanger for cracks or corrosion, testing the ignition system through its full sequence, cleaning the flame sensor, checking gas pressure at the manifold and supply, inspecting burners for carbon buildup, testing the inducer motor and pressure switch, and inspecting the flue and venting.

The technician documents every measurement and observation. When the inspection is complete, they walk the homeowner through their findings, explain what is operating well and what may need attention, and provide recommendations with estimated costs for any repairs that are not part of the standard tune-up scope. There is no obligation to approve any additional work.

This checklist-driven approach ensures nothing is missed and gives the homeowner a complete picture of their system's health.

For the indoor unit, the technician needs access to the air handler or furnace, which may be located in a closet, attic, garage, or utility room. If the unit is in the attic, a clear path with safe access is important.

The homeowner does not need to do anything special to prepare the system before the visit. The system should be in its normal operating state so the technician can observe real-world performance. If the system has been turned off for the season, the technician will start it up as part of the inspection.

After the visit, the technician reviews all findings and provides recommendations. If any additional work is suggested beyond the standard tune-up scope, it is presented as a separate recommendation with estimated costs. There is no obligation to approve anything beyond the scheduled maintenance.

Cancellation or rescheduling is straightforward. Calling at least 24 hours in advance allows the scheduling team to fill the slot and offer the caller a new appointment that works with their schedule.
